network.overall_strategy: This network constructs 10 distinct prebiotic pathways to L-alanine from simple geochemical feedstocks (CO2, H2, NH3, HCN, NO). Pyruvate serves as the central metabolic hub, produced independently via three non-circular upstream routes: (1) direct CO2+H2 Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on iron/magnetite/awaruite (Preiner et al., 2020), (2) lactic acid oxidation under cyanosulfidic conditions (Ritson, 2021), and (3) oxaloacetate decarboxylation where OAA itself derives from independent CO2 fixation (Muchowska et al., 2019). Acetaldehyde and formaldehyde serve as secondary hubs produced by FTT chemistry and pyruvate decarboxylation, feeding Strecker synthesis and serine construction pathways. Terminal steps to alanine include iron-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate with hydroxylamine (Muchowska et al., 2019), Strecker aminonitrile hydrolysis (Patel et al., 2015), and hydrothermal serine reduction (Aubrey et al., 2008). All intermediates have defined upstream synthesis routes, eliminating dangling precursors. Environmental flow proceeds Hydrothermal (CO2 fixation, reductive amination) ↔ Surface (Strecker, cyanosulfidic oxidation) → Biochemical (serine assembly).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 fixation to pyruvate
reactions.mechanism: H2-dependent CO2 reduction on native metal surfaces analogous to Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis. Iron and nickel-iron alloy (awaruite) surfaces activate H2 and reduce CO2 stepwise through C1 intermediates (formate, formaldehyde, CO) to C2 (acetate) and C3 (pyruvate) products. Magnetite provides an oxide surface that facilitates CO2 adsorption and reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated by Preiner, Igarashi, Muchowska, Yu, Varma, Kleinermanns, Nobu, Kamagata, Tüysüz, Moran, and Martin (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution, DOI: 10.1038/s41559-020-1125-6). They showed CO2 and H2 react on native iron, awaruite, and magnetite at 100°C to produce pyruvate and other metabolic intermediates. Three catalyst conditions were tested: (a) Fe⁰ + awaruite, (b) Fe⁰ + magnetite, (c) magnetite alone.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate with hydroxylamine

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: pyruvate + NH2OH → alanine + 2H2O. Mild hydrothermal conditions at alkaline vent mixing zones.

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted reductive amination where hydroxylamine acts as the nitrogen donor and iron species (Fe⁰, Fe²⁺) provide the reducing equivalents to drive the transamination. The α-keto acid (pyruvate) is converted to the corresponding α-amino acid (alanine). Fe⁰ donates electrons while Fe²⁺ acts as Lewis acid to coordinate substrates.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1). They showed that metallic iron and ferrous iron at 70°C in water promote interconversion of α-keto acids and α-amino acids via reductive amination with hydroxylamine, specifically pyruvate → alanine. This is part of an iron-driven geochemical analog of core biological metabolism.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 reduction to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: 2CO2 + 4H2 → CH3CHO + 3H2O. Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis produces oxygenated C2 products including acetaldehyde.

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced stepwise on magnetite surfaces: CO2 → CO → HCHO → CH3OH or CH3CHO. Iron oxide surfaces promote CO2 adsorption and sequential hydrogenation. C-C bond formation via CO insertion or formaldehyde coupling yields the C2 aldehyde. This is a well-known product class of FTT synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on iron oxide minerals is well-established to produce C1-C3 oxygenates including aldehydes (McCollom & Seewald, 2007, Chemical Reviews). Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated CO2+H2 chemistry on magnetite/Fe⁰ produces metabolic intermediates. Acetaldehyde is a recognized FTT product and serves as the critical bridge from hydrothermal CO2 fixation to surface Strecker chemistry. Acetaldehyde also forms from pyruvate thermal decomposition (see rxn_010), making it a convergence point.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of 2-aminopropanenitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Strecker synthesis in evaporitic pools. CH3CHO + NH3 + HCN → CH3CH(NH2)CN + H2O

reactions.mechanism: Three-step one-pot Strecker synthesis: (1) Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to acetaldehyde forms an α-aminoalcohol, which dehydrates to an imine (aldimine, CH3CH=NH). (2) Nucleophilic addition of cyanide (from HCN) to the imine C=N bond yields the α-aminonitrile, 2-aminopropanenitrile. Montmorillonite clay concentrates reactants on its interlayer surfaces and provides Lewis acid catalysis for imine formation.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the classical prebiotic route to aminonitriles (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) identified 2-aminopropanenitrile as the key intermediate in the cyanosulfidic route to alanine. Clay minerals such as montmorillonite are well-documented to concentrate and catalyze prebiotic condensations (Ferris et al., 1996, Origins of Life).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 2-aminopropanenitrile to L-alanine

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: CH3CH(NH2)CN + 2H2O → CH3CH(NH2)COOH + NH3. UV exposure may assist. Cyanosulfidic pool conditions.

reactions.mechanism: Two-step nitrile hydrolysis: (1) Nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile group (C≡N) of 2-aminopropanenitrile forms an amide intermediate (2-aminopropanamide). (2) Further hydrolysis of the amide yields the carboxylic acid (alanine) with release of ammonia. Clay surfaces may catalyze hydrolysis and UV exposure can promote the initial nucleophilic attack.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated within the cyanosulfidic protometabolism framework by Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The hydrolysis of α-aminonitriles to α-amino acids is a well-established reaction in aqueous prebiotic chemistry. The NH3 released is recycled as a hub molecule for further Strecker reactions.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive dehydroxylation of L-serine to L-alanine

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: HOCH2CH(NH2)COOH + H2 → CH3CH(NH2)COOH + H2O. H2 from serpentinization. Submarine hydrothermal vent conditions.

reactions.mechanism: Reductive dehydroxylation: the hydroxymethyl group (-CH2OH) of serine is reduced to a methyl group (-CH3). Molecular hydrogen (H2), abundant in hydrothermal vent fluids from serpentinization, serves as the reductant. Iron sulfide mineral surfaces (pyrite, mackinawite) facilitate H2 activation and mediate electron transfer to the substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2). They showed L-serine is reduced to L-alanine by molecular hydrogen under submarine hydrothermal conditions, expanding the prebiotic amino acid inventory through hydrothermal interconversion.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: acetaldehyde + HCN → lactonitrile

conditions.other: Cyanohydrin reaction: CH3CHO + HCN → CH3CH(OH)CN. Occurs readily in aqueous solution at mildly basic pH.

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide ion (CN⁻, from HCN dissociation) to the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde, forming the cyanohydrin (2-hydroxypropanenitrile / lactonitrile). The reaction is thermodynamically favorable and proceeds at ambient temperature. Clay surfaces provide mild base catalysis and concentrate reactants.
reactions.reasoning: The cyanohydrin reaction is a fundamental prebiotic transformation providing α-hydroxy nitriles as precursors to α-hydroxy acids. This is the first step of the alternative pathway to lactic acid, which Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981) showed can be oxidized to pyruvate under cyanosulfidic conditions. Clay minerals catalyze such reactions (Ferris, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of lactonitrile to lactic acid

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: CH3CH(OH)CN + 2H2O → CH3CH(OH)COOH + NH3. Nitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide intermediate.

reactions.mechanism: Two-step nitrile hydrolysis analogous to rxn_005: (1) Water attacks the nitrile carbon of lactonitrile to form 2-hydroxypropanamide (lactamide). (2) Further hydrolysis cleaves the amide to yield lactic acid and ammonia. Mineral surfaces catalyze the hydrolysis and the released NH3 is recycled into the Strecker network.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to the corresponding acid is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. This reaction provides the upstream source for lactic acid, addressing the dangling precursor problem identified in previous network attempts. Lactic acid then feeds into the cyanosulfidic oxidation to pyruvate (Ritson, 2021, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981). Clay and phosphate minerals are known to catalyze hydrolysis reactions in surface environments.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic oxidation of lactic acid to pyruvate

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic oxidation: CH3CH(OH)COOH → CH3C(=O)COOH. Sulfur species act as oxidants. ~1 hour reaction time.

reactions.mechanism: Oxidation of the α-hydroxy group of lactic acid to a ketone, converting lactate to pyruvate. Under cyanosulfidic conditions, reactive sulfur species (from H2S/HCN-derived thio-compounds) serve as the terminal oxidant. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable and drives the α-hydroxy acid toward the α-keto acid.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981) as part of a cyanosulfidic origin of the Krebs cycle. Lactic acid is oxidized to pyruvate under surface cyanosulfidic conditions, providing an additional prebiotic route to the central pyruvate hub from α-hydroxy acid precursors.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Pyruvate → acetaldehyde + CO2. Thermally driven non-oxidative decarboxylation. This is a known instability of pyruvate at hydrothermal temperatures.

reactions.mechanism: Non-oxidative decarboxylation of the α-keto acid: the carboxyl group adjacent to the ketone is lost as CO2, producing the corresponding aldehyde (acetaldehyde). This reaction is thermodynamically favorable at elevated temperatures and proceeds on mineral surfaces. Iron oxide and sulfide minerals facilitate the decarboxylation through Lewis acid coordination of the keto-acid moiety.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate thermal instability is well-documented in the prebiotic chemistry literature. The literature review explicitly notes: 'Pyruvate is thermally labile and can decarboxylate to acetaldehyde + CO2 at elevated temperatures.' This reaction creatively converts a potential degradation pathway into a productive link, generating acetaldehyde for downstream Strecker synthesis. This bridges hydrothermal pyruvate pools to surface aminonitrile chemistry.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: CO2 + 2H2 → HCHO + H2O. Formaldehyde is a C1 intermediate in FTT synthesis; it can be trapped and transported from vent environments.

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise CO2 reduction: CO2 is first reduced to CO on iron surfaces (reverse water-gas shift), then CO is hydrogenated to formaldehyde (HCHO). This is the earliest C1 intermediate in the Fischer-Tropsch chain. On magnetite surfaces, formaldehyde can accumulate as a transient product before further reduction or release into hydrothermal fluids.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde is a recognized intermediate in Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on iron oxide surfaces. Preiner et al. (2020, DOI: 10.1038/s41559-020-1125-6) demonstrated CO2 + H2 chemistry on magnetite/Fe⁰ producing various organic products. While formaldehyde is typically a transient intermediate, it can be stabilized by rapid transport away from the high-T zone or by reaction with dissolved species. This reaction provides the essential C1 building block for the formose-like and Strecker glycine pathways.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde

conditions.other: Strecker synthesis: HCHO + NH3 + HCN → H2NCH2CN + H2O. The simplest Strecker reaction for glycine precursor.

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form methyleneimine (H2C=NH), which is attacked by cyanide ion to yield aminoacetonitrile (glycinonitrile). Clay mineral surfaces concentrate the reactants and provide Lewis acid catalysis for the imine formation step. This is the Strecker route to the simplest amino acid (glycine) precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN is one of the oldest and best-established prebiotic reactions (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). It was central to Miller-Urey chemistry and confirmed by Patel et al. (2015, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) as part of the cyanosulfidic protometabolism framework. Montmorillonite catalysis of such condensations is well-documented (Ferris et al., 1996).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: H2NCH2CN + 2H2O → H2NCH2COOH + NH3. Proceeds through glycinamide intermediate.

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydrolysis: (1) Water attacks the nitrile carbon of aminoacetonitrile to form glycinamide (H2NCH2CONH2). (2) Further hydrolysis of the amide yields glycine and ammonia. The reaction proceeds readily at ambient to mildly elevated temperatures. Mineral surfaces catalyze the hydrolysis and released NH3 is recycled.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amino acids via the Strecker pathway is a cornerstone of prebiotic amino acid synthesis (Miller, 1957). This provides the glycine needed for serine construction in rxn_014. The complete Strecker route (aldehyde → aminonitrile → amino acid) is experimentally well-validated under prebiotic surface conditions.

reactions.name: Hydroxymethylation of glycine to L-serine

conditions.other: Aldol-type condensation: H2NCH2COOH + HCHO → HOCH2CH(NH2)COOH. Clay surface provides catalysis and concentration.

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type condensation: formaldehyde attacks the α-carbon of glycine. The α-amino group activates the adjacent carbon toward nucleophilic attack on the electrophilic formaldehyde carbonyl. The product is serine, bearing a hydroxymethyl side chain. Clay mineral surfaces provide Lewis acid catalysis and concentrate the reactants through adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: The condensation of glycine with formaldehyde to form serine is the prebiotic analog of the biological serine hydroxymethyltransferase reaction. While this reaction is more facile with enzymatic catalysis in modern biology, clay-mineral-catalyzed versions are plausible (Lambert, 2008, Origins of Life). This provides the complete upstream route to L-serine, which is then reduced to alanine (Aubrey et al., 2008). Classified as 'Biochemical' as it represents prebiotic pool chemistry on mineral surfaces.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 fixation to oxaloacetate

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: 4CO2 + 6H2 → OAA + 4H2O. Extended FTT producing C4 keto-diacid. Serpentinization conditions.

reactions.mechanism: Extended Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 fixation: sequential hydrogenation and C-C coupling on native metal surfaces produces C4 keto-acid (oxaloacetate). The reaction proceeds through C1 → C2 → C3 → C4 intermediates via CO insertion and aldol-type condensations on the metal/oxide surface. Awaruite and Fe⁰ activate H2 while magnetite facilitates CO2 adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020, DOI: 10.1038/s41559-020-1125-6) demonstrated that CO2 + H2 on native iron, awaruite, and magnetite at 100°C produces pyruvate and other organic acids. Muchowska et al. (2019, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) showed that the iron-promoted reaction network includes TCA cycle intermediates such as oxaloacetate. The production of C4 keto-diacids from CO2 is a plausible extension of the FTT framework. This provides an independent (non-circular) upstream source for OAA, avoiding the fatal pyruvate→OAA→pyruvate cycle identified in previous attempts.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate

conditions.other: OAA → pyruvate + CO2. β-decarboxylation catalyzed by Fe²⁺ Lewis acid.

reactions.mechanism: β-Decarboxylation of oxaloacetate catalyzed by Fe²⁺ ions. Iron(II) acts as a Lewis acid to coordinate the β-keto acid and facilitate CO2 loss. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable and proceeds readily at mild hydrothermal temperatures (70°C). This produces pyruvate as a stable C3 product.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1). They showed that Fe²⁺ catalyzes the decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate at 70°C in water, as part of an iron-driven geochemical analog of the Krebs cycle. This is one of the most thermodynamically favorable reactions in the network.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate with ammonia

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: pyruvate + NH3 + H2 → alanine + H2O. Reductive amination using ammonia as N-donor and H2 as reductant.

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination: ammonia attacks the ketone carbonyl of pyruvate to form an imine (Schiff base, CH3C(=NH)COOH). The imine is then reduced by H2 (activated on iron/iron sulfide surfaces) to yield alanine. Greigite (Fe3S4) provides an Fe-S cluster surface that activates both H2 and the imine for reduction, analogous to [Fe4S4] clusters in biological transamination enzymes.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids with NH3 and a reductant is a well-known reaction in prebiotic chemistry. While Muchowska et al. (2019, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) specifically demonstrated hydroxylamine as the N-donor, the use of ammonia (far more abundant prebiotically) with H2 as a co-reductant on iron mineral surfaces is a plausible extrapolation supported by general reductive amination chemistry. Greigite is the prebiotic analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters and is known to catalyze reductions (Russell & Hall, 1997). This reaction provides an alternative terminal step using the more prebiotically abundant NH3.

reactions.name: Reduction of nitric oxide to hydroxylamine by metallic iron

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: NO + H2 → NH2OH. Reduction of volcanic NO by iron-activated hydrogen.

reactions.mechanism: Nitric oxide (NO), produced by volcanic lightning and outgassing, is reduced by H2 activated on metallic iron and iron sulfide surfaces. The reaction proceeds: NO is adsorbed on the Fe⁰ surface, H2 is dissociatively chemisorbed, and sequential hydrogenation produces HNO → NHOH → NH2OH (hydroxylamine). The partially reduced product (NH2OH) is kinetically favored over full reduction to NH3 at moderate temperatures.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review states that hydroxylamine 'could form from NO reduction by Fe⁰' (Muchowska et al., 2019 discussion). Volcanic NO is a recognized prebiotic nitrogen source (Navarro-González et al., 2001, Nature). Fe⁰ is a strong reductant for N-oxides, and partial reduction to NH2OH (rather than full reduction to NH3) is thermodynamically and kinetically plausible. This reaction provides the essential upstream source for hydroxylamine, addressing the dangling precursor identified in previous network attempts.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal CO2-to-alanine via pyruvate + hydroxylamine
pathways.description: The primary hydrothermal pathway: CO2 and H2 are fixed to pyruvate on Fe⁰/awaruite/magnetite via Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry (Preiner et al., 2020), then pyruvate undergoes iron-catalyzed reductive amination with hydroxylamine (Muchowska et al., 2019) to yield alanine. Hydroxylamine is produced by NO reduction with H2 on Fe⁰. Entirely hydrothermal.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal CO2-to-alanine via pyruvate + ammonia
pathways.description: Alternative hydrothermal pathway using ammonia as the nitrogen donor instead of hydroxylamine. CO2 + H2 → pyruvate (Preiner et al., 2020), then reductive amination with NH3 + H2 on iron/greigite surfaces yields alanine. Entirely hydrothermal, using the more abundant ammonia.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-Surface Strecker pathway via FTT acetaldehyde
pathways.description: CO2 + H2 → acetaldehyde via FTT (hydrothermal), then acetaldehyde is transported to surface pools where Strecker synthesis with NH3 + HCN yields 2-aminopropanenitrile, which hydrolyzes to alanine (Patel et al., 2015). Clear hydrothermal → surface environmental transition.

pathways.name: Lactic acid route: FTT acetaldehyde → lactonitrile → lactic acid → pyruvate → alanine (hydroxylamine)
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to acetaldehyde (FTT), which undergoes cyanohydrin reaction with HCN → lactonitrile (surface), hydrolysis → lactic acid, cyanosulfidic oxidation → pyruvate (Ritson, 2021), then reductive amination → alanine (Muchowska et al., 2019). Crosses hydrothermal → surface → hydrothermal environments.

pathways.name: Serine reduction pathway: CO2 → formaldehyde → glycine → serine → alanine
pathways.description: Full chain from CO2 to alanine via the serine route. CO2 → formaldehyde (FTT, hydrothermal), formaldehyde → aminoacetonitrile (Strecker, surface), hydrolysis → glycine (surface), glycine + formaldehyde → serine (biochemical pool), serine + H2 → alanine (hydrothermal, Aubrey et al., 2008). Spans all three environments.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation bridge to Strecker: CO2 → pyruvate → acetaldehyde → alanine
pathways.description: CO2 + H2 → pyruvate (FTT, Preiner et al., 2020), pyruvate thermally decarboxylates to acetaldehyde + CO2 at hydrothermal temperatures, then acetaldehyde is transported to surface pools for Strecker synthesis → 2-aminopropanenitrile → alanine (Patel et al., 2015). Creatively uses pyruvate instability as a productive bridge.

pathways.name: Oxaloacetate route: CO2 → OAA → pyruvate → alanine (hydroxylamine)
pathways.description: Independent CO2 fixation produces oxaloacetate via extended FTT (Preiner et al., 2020 framework), Fe²⁺-catalyzed decarboxylation yields pyruvate (Muchowska et al., 2019), then reductive amination with hydroxylamine → alanine. No circularity: OAA derives from CO2, not from pyruvate.

pathways.name: Oxaloacetate route: CO2 → OAA → pyruvate → alanine (ammonia)
pathways.description: Same OAA upstream as P7, but terminal reductive amination uses NH3 + H2 instead of hydroxylamine. CO2 → OAA (FTT) → pyruvate (Fe²⁺ decarboxylation) → alanine (NH3 reductive amination on greigite).

pathways.name: OAA → pyruvate → acetaldehyde → Strecker → alanine
pathways.description: Longest cross-environment pathway: CO2 → OAA (FTT, hydrothermal), OAA → pyruvate (Fe²⁺ decarboxylation, hydrothermal), pyruvate → acetaldehyde (thermal decarboxylation, hydrothermal), acetaldehyde → 2-aminopropanenitrile (Strecker, surface), hydrolysis → alanine (surface). Passes through both hub molecules.

pathways.name: Lactic acid route with ammonia terminus: acetaldehyde → lactic acid → pyruvate → alanine (NH3)
pathways.description: Same upstream as P4 (FTT acetaldehyde → cyanohydrin → lactonitrile → lactic acid → pyruvate via Ritson cyanosulfidic oxidation), but terminal step uses NH3 + H2 reductive amination instead of hydroxylamine. Demonstrates redundancy in both upstream and downstream legs.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong 3-step pathway. FTT pyruvate synthesis (Preiner et al. 2020) and iron-catalyzed reductive amination with hydroxylamine (Muchowska et al. 2019) are both directly literature-supported. Hydroxylamine sourcing via NO reduction on metallic iron (rxn_018) is plausible but less experimentally constrained. All steps hydrothermal, consistent environment.
suggestions: Cite specific experimental evidence for NO reduction to hydroxylamine on Fe0; consider specifying temperature and mineral surfaces more precisely for rxn_018

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Elegant 2-step pathway. Pyruvate from FTT chemistry is well-supported. Reductive amination with ammonia (rxn_017) is chemically reasonable and an extension of the Muchowska et al. framework, though the primary literature demonstrates hydroxylamine rather than ammonia as the nitrogen source. The use of H2 as reductant with Fe0 catalyst is plausible for driving the NH3 reductive amination.
suggestions: Distinguish more carefully between the hydroxylamine-based reductive amination (directly demonstrated) and ammonia-based reductive amination (reasonable but less directly cited)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent Strecker pathway. FTT acetaldehyde production (rxn_003) is plausible as C2 products are common FTT outputs, though less directly cited than pyruvate. Strecker synthesis (rxn_004) and aminonitrile hydrolysis (rxn_005) are classic prebiotic chemistry supported by Patel et al. 2015. Environmental progression from Hydrothermal (FTT) to Surface (Strecker) is well-motivated and realistic.
suggestions: Could strengthen rxn_003 by citing specific FTT experimental evidence for acetaldehyde production

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Viable but long 6-step route. The cyanohydrin → lactic acid → pyruvate sequence adds prebiotic plausibility (Ritson 2021 supports lactic acid oxidation). However, the environmental flow is less ideal: Hydrothermal → Surface → Surface → Surface → Hydrothermal → Hydrothermal requires transport of intermediates back to hydrothermal environments. Each individual reaction is sound but the overall pathway length and environmental back-and-forth reduce confidence.
suggestions: Consider whether the surface-to-hydrothermal transport of pyruvate is realistic given pyruvate thermal lability

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interesting pathway linking formaldehyde through glycine and serine to alanine. The weakest link is rxn_014 (glycine hydroxymethylation to serine), which in biology requires serine hydroxymethyltransferase and pyridoxal phosphate cofactor. Prebiotic analogs of this reaction are less well-established. Serine reduction to alanine (rxn_006) is supported by Aubrey et al. 2008. The Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical → Hydrothermal environmental flow is complex.
suggestions: Provide prebiotic evidence for glycine hydroxymethylation to serine without enzymatic catalysis; consider mineral-mediated aldol-type condensation as a mechanism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent pathway that cleverly bridges FTT pyruvate synthesis with Strecker chemistry via pyruvate decarboxylation. All reactions are well-supported: pyruvate from CO2+H2 (Preiner et al. 2020), thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde (acknowledged as a challenge in the literature), and Strecker synthesis + hydrolysis (Patel et al. 2015). The pyruvate decarboxylation that is normally a problem becomes productive here. Environmental flow Hydrothermal → Surface is natural.
suggestions: Note that pyruvate thermal lability, often seen as a liability, is exploited constructively here

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Viable pathway through oxaloacetate. The weakest link is rxn_015 (FTT CO2+H2 to oxaloacetate), as direct FTT synthesis of a C4 dicarboxylic keto acid is more challenging than C3 pyruvate. Preiner et al. 2020 demonstrated some C4 products but OAA specifically at useful yields is less established. OAA decarboxylation to pyruvate (rxn_016) is well-supported by Muchowska et al. 2019.
suggestions: Strengthen the OAA formation step; consider alternative routes to OAA such as CO2 carboxylation of pyruvate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Clean 3-step pathway but shares the OAA formation weakness of P7. All hydrothermal environment is consistent. The ammonia reductive amination terminal step (rxn_017) is reasonable but less directly cited than the hydroxylamine version.
suggestions: Same as P7 regarding OAA formation; consider combining with better OAA upstream routes

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Extended pathway from OAA through pyruvate decarboxylation to Strecker. The 5 steps make it longer but each reaction after the initial OAA formation is well-supported. The Hydrothermal → Surface environmental flow is natural. Shares the OAA formation weakness.
suggestions: Length could be reduced; this pathway is essentially P7/P8 + P6 combined

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The lactic acid route variant with ammonia terminus. Similar to P4 but slightly shorter by using NH3 instead of hydroxylamine. The environmental back-and-forth (Hydrothermal → Surface → Hydrothermal) is less ideal. Cyanosulfidic oxidation step (rxn_009, Ritson 2021) adds nice literature support.
suggestions: Environmental transport between surface cyanosulfidic conditions and hydrothermal reductive amination needs justification

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent, well-constructed prebiotic synthesis network for L-alanine with strong literature grounding. The network demonstrates sophisticated topology with 18 molecules, 18 reactions, and 10 distinct pathways showing multiple convergent routes to the target. Pyruvate as the central metabolic hub is perfectly aligned with the literature (Preiner et al. 2020, Muchowska et al. 2019). Key strengths: (1) All four experimentally demonstrated pathways from the literature review are incorporated (FTT pyruvate + hydroxylamine reductive amination, Strecker synthesis, serine reduction, CO2 fixation to pyruvate). (2) Hub molecules (pyruvate, acetaldehyde, formaldehyde, NH3, HCN) are well-connected with degree ≥ 3. (3) No dangling precursors - all intermediates have upstream synthesis routes. (4) The strategy explicitly addresses known challenges from the literature, such as pyruvate thermal lability (constructively exploited in P6 via decarboxylation to acetaldehyde). (5) Literature citations are accurate and well-integrated. Minor weaknesses: (1) rxn_014 (glycine → serine hydroxymethylation) lacks strong prebiotic evidence, weakening P5. (2) rxn_015 (FTT synthesis of oxaloacetate) is ambitious for direct CO2 fixation to a C4 compound. (3) The network correctly notes chirality as an unresolved issue. (4) Some environmental back-and-forth in longer pathways (P4, P10) could be better justified. Overall, this is a high-quality network that faithfully represents the current state of prebiotic alanine synthesis research.
